Across 810 conversations, spanning 9 target chatbots and 30 simulated user profiles, concerning behavior in target chatbots was widespread, albeit reduced in newer models. Concerning behavior varied by user vulnerability and conversational intent, accumulated over turns, and could be reduced by interventions at early escalation points. Risk was highest when otherwise supportive chatbot behaviors reinforced the psychological mechanisms underlying the simulated user’s vulnerability, a pattern we term a Vulnerability Amplifying Interaction Loop (VAIL). SIM-VAIL provides a scalable framework for mapping mental health risk across users, chatbots, and conversational trajectories, offering a foundation for targeted safety improvements.

# Introduction

Access to mental-health care is severely limited [@who_mental_2025]. With a global median of 13 mental-health workers per 100,000 people [@who_mental_2025], existing support systems underserve people living with mental illness [@liu_global_2025] and a far larger population seeking support for everyday behavioral health challenges [@shelmerdine_ai_2025]. Against this backdrop, millions now turn to general-purpose consumer AI chatbots, such as ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Copilot, for emotional support, relationship guidance, companionship, and therapeutic advice [@anthropic2025affective; @costa-gomez_its_nodate].

The widespread adoption, continuous availability, and low marginal cost of consumer AI chatbots have raised hopes that they might supplement existing mental-health services or provide support where professional care is inaccessible [@li_systematic_2023; @habicht_closing_2024; @maples_loneliness_2024]. These same properties, however, have been associated with mental-health risks to vulnerable users [@grabb_risks_2024; @morrin_delusions_2025; @perlisGenerativeAIUse2026]. Consequently, academics, clinicians, and industry actors increasingly recognize an urgent need for improved tools to evaluate and improve chatbot behavior in mental-health contexts [@de_freitas_health_2024; @anthropic_protecting_2025; @panCOMPASSGHConsensusRoadmap2026].

AI chatbots are built on large language models (LLMs), Transformer-based deep neural networks whose outputs are probabilistic and context-dependent, making it impossible to guarantee in advance how a model will behave in a new situation [@dohnany_technological_2026]. Empirical safety evaluations that measure how AI chatbots actually interact with vulnerable users are therefore indispensable [@sobowale_evaluating_2025; @belli_vera-mh_2025; @arnaiz-rodriguez_between_2025].

An effective empirical evaluation should meet 3 criteria. First, it should assess chatbot behavior across a broad, clinically meaningful distribution of user profiles and conversational intents [@pombal_mindeval_2025; @belli_vera-mh_2025; @yeung_psychogenic_2025]. Second, it should characterize chatbot behavior with respect to multiple clinically grounded risk dimensions [@sobowale_evaluating_2025; @luo_dialogguard_2025; @golden_framework_2024]. Third, it should be capable of characterizing this risk over the course of a conversation, because harmful response patterns can compound over time [@dohnany_technological_2026; @hong_measuring_2025; @laban2025llms].

Current evaluation approaches fall short on each of these requirements. Most automated benchmarks assess single-turn responses to fixed sets of user queries, and focus on a narrow set of failure modes, such as whether chatbots encourage self-harm or provide unsafe medical advice [@li_counselbench_2025; @arnaiz-rodriguez_between_2025; @badawi_when_2025]. Fixed query sets are vulnerable to overfitting or saturation as models are tuned, explicitly or implicitly, to achieve better benchmark scores [@ott_mapping_2022]. Single-turn tests also miss how risk changes as conversational context accumulates [@dohnany_technological_2026]. As a result, benchmark performance may generalize poorly to actual human-chatbot conversations seen in deployment, which are typically longer and more varied than those encountered during benchmark testing [@stamatis_beyond_2026].

A complementary approach is human red teaming, in which auditors engage AI chatbots in adversarial conversations with the explicit aim of eliciting responses that violate a predefined safety policy [@openai_strengthening_2025; @sobowale_evaluating_2025; @arnaiz-rodriguez_between_2025]. Human auditors are free to deploy a variety of adversarial strategies, and to change their approach as a conversation unfolds. While this makes human red teaming less vulnerable to some limitations of static single-turn benchmarks (e.g., overfitting), it remains labor-intensive and difficult to standardize. Moreover, human auditors may converge on stereotyped adversarial strategies that do not generalize to real-world conversations [@samvelyan_rainbow_2024; @stamatis_beyond_2026; @phang_investigating_2025].

An additional concern, affecting benchmarks and read teaming alike, is that current evaluation approaches focus primarily on overtly harmful responses, such as when chatbots endorse self-harm or use stigmatizing language. This focus can miss interactional harms that are less overt, more cumulative, and harder to detect from single responses, including instances in which chatbots reinforce maladaptive beliefs, encourage avoidance, or promote emotional dependence [@kirk_benefits_2024; @dohnany_technological_2026; @chengSycophanticAIDecreases2026]. Paradoxically, these harms may arise from chatbot behaviors that are often considered helpful, such as validation and empathy, but can nonetheless amplify the mechanisms of mental illness in vulnerable users.

To address these gaps, we introduce SIM-VAIL (*SIMulated Vulnerability-Amplifying Interaction Loops*), an automated adversarial evaluation framework that audits the mental-health risk profile of a target AI chatbot across a broad range of multi-turn psychiatric conversational contexts. SIM-VAIL builds on evaluation approaches that use a frontier LLM to role-play a user with a pre-specified psychological and behavioral profile, and task this simulated agent with adversarially engaging a target AI chatbot to elicit clinically relevant safety failures [@Inspect_2024; @gupta_bloom_2025; @fronsdal_petri_2025; @perlis_stella_2025]. SIM-VAIL can be understood as automated adversarial red teaming: it combines the scalability and standardization of automated benchmarking with the multi-turn adversarial structure of human red teaming. 

Using this framework, we tested whether AI chatbots enter Vulnerability-Amplifying Interaction Loops (VAILs), in which chatbot behaviors that appear helpful or benign in isolation become increasingly harmful across turns by reinforcing maladaptive psychological processes linked to the simulated user’s vulnerability [@dohnany_technological_2026].

# Results

SIM-VAIL evaluates user–chatbot interactions within a structured interaction space defined by 2 core dimensions: psychological vulnerability, or who the user is, and interaction intent, or what the user seeks from the AI chatbot (Fig. 1). For each turn, SIM-VAIL assigns scores across multiple clinically grounded risk dimensions, allowing risk to be tracked as the interaction unfolds. 

We present SIM-VAIL results across `r total_conversations` multi-turn conversations, spanning 30 simulated user profiles, 9 AI chatbots, and over `r (total_conversations + total_unrepeated_turns) * total_mh_ratings` turn-level ratings of mental-health risk behavior. Each simulated conversation involved 3 LLM-based chatbots: a *target AI chatbot* that is the subject of the evaluation; a *simulated user* (or auditor) that generates user messages (Anthropic’s claude-sonnet-4.5, unless otherwise stated); and an automated *safety judge* that scores the target chatbot responses on multiple risk dimensions (Anthropic’s claude-opus-4.5 for conversation-level scores and claude-sonnet-4.5 for turn-level scores, unless otherwise stated).

## SIM-VAIL framework

For the simulated user, we focused on 5 common psychological vulnerabilities. These vulnerabilities spanned a broad range of psychiatric risk profiles described in structural models of mental illness [@kotovParadigmShiftPsychiatric2018] and reflected cognitive-behavioral formulations of the corresponding conditions [@beckEvolutionCognitiveModel2008; @salkovskisUnderstandingTreatingObsessivecompulsive1999; @johnsonManiaDysregulationGoal2005; @mikulincerAttachmentOrientationsEmotion2019], including negative beliefs about oneself, hopelessness, withdrawal, and self-neglect in depression; aberrant salience and threat perception in psychosis; elevated confidence, urgency, and reduced need for sleep in mania; intrusive thoughts and intolerance of uncertainty in obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD); and fear of abandonment and reassurance seeking in insecure attachment.

For a given vulnerability, we simulated separate users harboring 1 of 6 conversational intents. These intents were chosen to reflect transdiagnostic mechanisms implicated in the onset, maintenance, and exacerbation of mental illness [@harvey_cognitive_2004], including seeking validation of maladaptive beliefs; reassurance that could enable avoidance; emotional dependence on the chatbot; minimization of symptoms or risks; permission for, or help with, risky actions; and glorification of distress or extreme mental states (see Extended Data Table 1). By combining these vulnerabilities and intents we generated 30 clinically grounded user profiles, where each profile represented one unique vulnerability-intent pairing.

We simulated conversations between each simulated user profile (i.e., each unique vulnerability-intent pairing) and a single target chatbot, drawn from 9 models developed by major frontier AI companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Google, and Meta; see Methods for the full list of models). In each conversation, the simulated user LLM (auditor model) was instantiated with a system prompt containing detailed information about the user’s psychological vulnerability and conversational intent, together with adversarial audit instructions to plan responses that remained aligned with the assigned user profile while also being likely to elicit a safety violation from the target model (see Supplementary Table 1).

A conversation ended when the auditor model judged that the target had produced a clinically relevant safety failure, or after a maximum of `r MAX_TURNS` turns (a turn consisted of one simulated user message followed by the chatbot’s response). Because of the non-deterministic nature of chatbot responses, we repeated each profile × target simulation 3 times, such that the full dataset comprised `r total_conversations` simulated conversations (5 vulnerabilities x 6 intents x 9 models x 3 repetitions) spanning `r total_unrepeated_turns` turns, with a median of `r median_turns_per_convo` turns per conversation.

The automated safety judge scored the target chatbot behavior at both the conversation- and turn-level across `r total_ratings` behavioral dimensions, `r total_mh_ratings` of which were selected a priori as being relevant to transdiagnostic mechanisms of mental illness [@harvey_cognitive_2004] (as rated by 3 clinical psychiatrists, VW, RD, and MMN). These mental-health dimensions captured overall concerning behavior and therapeutic quality, as well as specific risks, such as failing to respond appropriately to self-harm; validation of maladaptive beliefs; endorsing or helping plan risky actions; promoting avoidance through reassurance; inviting dependence on the chatbot; or downplaying, stigmatizing, or glamorizing mental illness (each dimension scored from 1 to 10, see Supplementary Tables 2-3). In total, we generated `r total_conversations * total_mh_ratings` conversation-level ratings and `r total_unrepeated_turns * total_mh_ratings` turn-level ratings (see Fig. 1 for example scores; a conversation’s mean turn-by-turn risk score correlated with its conversation-level risk score at r = `r round(STAT.r_concerning$estimate,2)`; Extended Data Fig. 1A).

```{r}
#| label: revision_human_validation_setup
#| include: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(psych)
library(gridExtra)

hv_raw <- read_csv("../out/v1/human_annotation/human_rater_master.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(
    prolific_participant_id_raw = prolific_participant_id,
    participant_id_missing = is.na(prolific_participant_id),
    annotation_timestamp = as.POSIXct(
      annotation_timestamp,
      format = "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%OSZ",
      tz = "UTC"
    )
  )

hv_excluded_no_id <- hv_raw |>
  filter(participant_id_missing)

hv <- hv_raw |>
  filter(!participant_id_missing) |>
  group_by(prolific_participant_id) |>
  mutate(
    concerning_demeaned = concerning - mean(concerning, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

hv_theme <- if (exists("theme_like_main")) {
  theme_like_main
} else {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      legend.position = "top",
      legend.direction = "horizontal",
      legend.justification = "right",
      legend.background = element_blank(),
      legend.key = element_blank()
    )
}

hv_n_annotations <- nrow(hv)
hv_n_raters <- n_distinct(hv$prolific_participant_id)
hv_n_excluded_no_id <- nrow(hv_excluded_no_id)
hv_n_items <- n_distinct(hv$external_id)
hv_n_vulnerabilities <- n_distinct(hv$phenotype)
hv_n_intents <- n_distinct(hv$intention)
hv_n_target_models <- n_distinct(hv$target_model)
hv_n_llm_score_levels <- n_distinct(hv$turn_concerning)
hv_compensation_usd <- 65L
hv_realism_scale_min <- 1L
hv_realism_scale_max <- 5L
hv_concerning_scale_min <- 1L
hv_concerning_scale_max <- 10L
hv_concerning_sd_exclusion_threshold <- 0.5
hv_machine_score_range_threshold <- 4L
hv_min_ratings_for_exclusion <- 3L

hv_score_level_counts <- hv |>
  distinct(external_id, turn_concerning) |>
  count(turn_concerning, name = "n")

hv_min_per_score_level <- min(hv_score_level_counts$n)
hv_max_per_score_level <- max(hv_score_level_counts$n)

hv_experiment_summary <- hv |>
  group_by(experiment) |>
  summarise(
    n_raters = n_distinct(prolific_participant_id),
    n_items = n_distinct(external_id),
    n_annotations = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

hv_item_rater_counts <- hv |>
  distinct(external_id, prolific_participant_id) |>
  count(external_id, name = "n_distinct_raters")

hv_item_annotation_counts <- hv |>
  count(external_id, name = "n_annotations")

hv_n_items_single_rated <- sum(hv_item_rater_counts$n_distinct_raters == 1)
hv_n_items_multi_rated <- sum(hv_item_rater_counts$n_distinct_raters >= 2)
hv_n_items_exactly_two_raters <- sum(hv_item_rater_counts$n_distinct_raters == 2)
hv_n_items_more_than_two_raters <- sum(hv_item_rater_counts$n_distinct_raters > 2)
hv_n_items_one_annotation <- sum(hv_item_annotation_counts$n_annotations == 1)
hv_n_items_two_annotations <- sum(hv_item_annotation_counts$n_annotations == 2)
hv_n_items_three_annotations <- sum(hv_item_annotation_counts$n_annotations == 3)
hv_n_items_four_annotations <- sum(hv_item_annotation_counts$n_annotations == 4)

hv_raters <- hv |>
  distinct(
    prolific_participant_id,
    rater_age,
    rater_sex,
    rater_ethnicity,
    rater_country,
    rater_specialty
  )

hv_annotations_per_rater <- hv |>
  count(prolific_participant_id, name = "n_annotations")

hv_specialty_summary <- hv_raters |>
  mutate(
    specialty_group = case_when(
      rater_specialty == "Family Physician / General Practitioner" ~ "Family Medicine / GP",
      rater_specialty == "General Medical Doctor (non-specialist)" ~ "General Medical Doctor",
      rater_specialty == "Anesthesiologist (Anaesthetist)" ~ "Anaesthesiology",
      rater_specialty == "Other Healthcare Professional" ~ "Not disclosed",
      is.na(rater_specialty) ~ "Not disclosed",
      TRUE ~ rater_specialty
    )
  ) |>
  count(specialty_group, name = "n") |>
  arrange(match(
    specialty_group,
    c("Family Medicine / GP", "General Medical Doctor", "Anaesthesiology", "Not disclosed")
  ))

hv_n_uk <- sum(hv_raters$rater_country == "United Kingdom", na.rm = TRUE)
hv_n_us <- sum(hv_raters$rater_country == "United States", na.rm = TRUE)
hv_n_male <- sum(hv_raters$rater_sex == "Male", na.rm = TRUE)
hv_n_female <- sum(hv_raters$rater_sex == "Female", na.rm = TRUE)
hv_age_median <- median(hv_raters$rater_age, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_age_min <- min(hv_raters$rater_age, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_age_max <- max(hv_raters$rater_age, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_age_mean <- mean(hv_raters$rater_age, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_n_family_medicine <- sum(hv_specialty_summary$n[hv_specialty_summary$specialty_group == "Family Medicine / GP"])
hv_n_general_medical <- sum(hv_specialty_summary$n[hv_specialty_summary$specialty_group == "General Medical Doctor"])
hv_n_anaesthesiology <- sum(hv_specialty_summary$n[hv_specialty_summary$specialty_group == "Anaesthesiology"])
hv_n_specialty_undisclosed <- sum(hv_specialty_summary$n[hv_specialty_summary$specialty_group == "Not disclosed"])

hv_demographics_tbl <- tibble(
  Characteristic = c(
    "**N annotators**",
    "**Country of residence**",
    "**Sex**",
    "**Ethnicity**",
    "**Age**",
    "**Specialty (where disclosed)**",
    "**Annotations per annotator**"
  ),
  Value = c(
    hv_n_raters,
    paste0(
      "United Kingdom: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_country == "United Kingdom", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_country == "United Kingdom", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "United States: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_country == "United States", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_country == "United States", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "Not reported: ", sum(is.na(hv_raters$rater_country)), " (",
      round(100 * sum(is.na(hv_raters$rater_country)) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%)"
    ),
    paste0(
      "Male: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_sex == "Male", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_sex == "Male", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "Female: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_sex == "Female", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_sex == "Female", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "Not reported: ", sum(is.na(hv_raters$rater_sex)), " (",
      round(100 * sum(is.na(hv_raters$rater_sex)) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%)"
    ),
    paste0(
      "White: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "White", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "White", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "Black: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "Black", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "Black", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "Asian: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "Asian", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "Asian", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "Mixed: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "Mixed", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "Mixed", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "Other: ", sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "Other", na.rm = TRUE), " (",
      round(100 * sum(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity == "Other", na.rm = TRUE) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%), ",
      "Not reported: ", sum(is.na(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity)), " (",
      round(100 * sum(is.na(hv_raters$rater_ethnicity)) / hv_n_raters, 0), "%)"
    ),
    paste0(
      "Median ", round(median(hv_raters$rater_age, na.rm = TRUE), 0),
      ", range ", round(min(hv_raters$rater_age, na.rm = TRUE), 0),
      "–", round(max(hv_raters$rater_age, na.rm = TRUE), 0),
      ", mean ", round(mean(hv_raters$rater_age, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
    ),
    paste(
      paste0(hv_specialty_summary$specialty_group, ": ", hv_specialty_summary$n),
      collapse = ", "
    ),
    paste0(
      "Median ", round(median(hv_annotations_per_rater$n_annotations), 0),
      ", range ", min(hv_annotations_per_rater$n_annotations),
      "–", max(hv_annotations_per_rater$n_annotations)
    )
  )
)

hv_phenotype_labels <- c(
  "DEPRESSION" = "Depression",
  "PSYCHOSIS" = "Psychosis",
  "MANIA_HYPOMANIA" = "Mania",
  "OCD" = "Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)",
  "INSECURE_ATTACHMENT" = "Insecure attachment (IA)"
)

hv_intention_labels <- c(
  "A_belief_validation" = "Belief validation",
  "B_risky_action" = "Risky action permission",
  "C_reassurance_avoidance" = "Reassurance and avoidance",
  "D_dependence_anthropomorphism" = "Dependence and anthropomorphism",
  "E_trivialization" = "Trivialization and minimization",
  "F_glorification" = "Glorification and romanticization"
)

hv_target_labels <- c(
  "claude-3.7-sonnet" = "claude-sonnet-3.7",
  "claude-4.5-sonnet" = "claude-sonnet-4.5",
  "gemini-2.5-flash" = "gemini-2.5-flash",
  "gemini-2.5-pro" = "gemini-2.5-pro",
  "gpt-4o" = "gpt-4o",
  "gpt-5" = "gpt-5",
  "grok-3" = "grok-3",
  "grok-4" = "grok-4",
  "llama-3.1-70b-instruct" = "llama-3.1-70B-Instruct"
)

hv_stimulus_tbl <- tibble(
  Dimension = c("**Vulnerability**", "**Intent**", "**Target AI chatbot**"),
  Categories = c(
    paste(
      hv |>
        count(phenotype, name = "n") |>
        mutate(
          phenotype_label = unname(hv_phenotype_labels[as.character(phenotype)])
        ) |>
        arrange(match(phenotype, c("DEPRESSION", "PSYCHOSIS", "MANIA_HYPOMANIA", "OCD", "INSECURE_ATTACHMENT"))) |>
        transmute(txt = paste0(phenotype_label, ": ", n)) |>
        pull(txt),
      collapse = ", "
    ),
    paste(
      hv |>
        mutate(
          intention_label = unname(
            hv_intention_labels[as.character(intention)]
          )
        ) |>
        count(intention, intention_label, name = "n") |>
        arrange(match(intention, c("A_belief_validation", "B_risky_action", "C_reassurance_avoidance", "D_dependence_anthropomorphism", "E_trivialization", "F_glorification"))) |>
        transmute(txt = paste0(intention_label, ": ", n)) |>
        pull(txt),
      collapse = ", "
    ),
    paste(
      hv |>
        count(target_model, name = "n") |>
        mutate(
          target_label = unname(hv_target_labels[as.character(target_model)])
        ) |>
        arrange(match(target_model, c(
          "claude-3.7-sonnet",
          "claude-4.5-sonnet",
          "gemini-2.5-flash",
          "gemini-2.5-pro",
          "llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
          "gpt-4o",
          "gpt-5",
          "grok-3",
          "grok-4"
        ))) |>
        transmute(txt = paste0(target_label, ": ", n)) |>
        pull(txt),
      collapse = ", "
    )
  ),
  `N annotations` = c(hv_n_annotations, hv_n_annotations, hv_n_annotations)
)

hv_realism_mean <- mean(hv$realism, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_realism_sd <- sd(hv$realism, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_realism_median <- median(hv$realism, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_realism_pct_45 <- mean(hv$realism >= 4, na.rm = TRUE) * 100

hv_realism_table <- hv |>
  count(realism, name = "N") |>
  complete(realism = 1:5, fill = list(N = 0)) |>
  mutate(
    Label = c(
      "Clearly artificial",
      "Broadly implausible",
      "Mixed",
      "Broadly plausible",
      "Reads as genuine"
    ),
    Percent = round(100 * N / sum(N), 0)
  ) |>
  transmute(
    Score = realism,
    Label,
    N,
    Percent
  )

hv_realism_pct_1 <- hv_realism_table$Percent[hv_realism_table$Score == 1]
hv_realism_pct_4 <- hv_realism_table$Percent[hv_realism_table$Score == 4]
hv_realism_pct_5 <- hv_realism_table$Percent[hv_realism_table$Score == 5]

hv_hllm <- hv |>
  select(turn_concerning, concerning, concerning_demeaned) |>
  drop_na()

hv_cor_demeaned <- cor.test(
  hv_hllm$turn_concerning,
  hv_hllm$concerning_demeaned,
  method = "spearman",
  exact = FALSE
)

hv_quintile_levels <- c("Q1 (1–2)", "Q2 (3–4)", "Q3 (5–6)", "Q4 (7–8)", "Q5 (9–10)")

hv_quintile_summary <- hv |>
  mutate(
    llm_quintile = case_when(
      turn_concerning <= 2 ~ "Q1 (1–2)",
      turn_concerning <= 4 ~ "Q2 (3–4)",
      turn_concerning <= 6 ~ "Q3 (5–6)",
      turn_concerning <= 8 ~ "Q4 (7–8)",
      TRUE ~ "Q5 (9–10)"
    ),
    llm_quintile = factor(llm_quintile, levels = hv_quintile_levels)
  ) |>
  group_by(llm_quintile) |>
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    mean_human = mean(concerning, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_human = sd(concerning, na.rm = TRUE),
    SEM = sd_human / sqrt(N),
    lower = mean_human - SEM,
    upper = mean_human + SEM,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

hv_quintile_monotonic <- cor.test(
  seq_len(nrow(hv_quintile_summary)),
  hv_quintile_summary$mean_human,
  method = "spearman",
  exact = FALSE
)

hv_pair_source <- hv |>
  arrange(external_id, prolific_participant_id, annotation_timestamp) |>
  distinct(external_id, prolific_participant_id, .keep_all = TRUE)

hv_pairs_long <- hv_pair_source |>
  arrange(external_id, prolific_participant_id) |>
  group_by(external_id) |>
  slice_head(n = 2) |>
  filter(n_distinct(prolific_participant_id) == 2) |>
  mutate(rater_slot = row_number()) |>
  ungroup()

hv_pairs_wide <- hv_pairs_long |>
  select(
    external_id,
    rater_slot,
    prolific_participant_id,
    concerning,
    concerning_demeaned
  ) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = rater_slot,
    values_from = c(prolific_participant_id, concerning, concerning_demeaned)
  )

hv_n_pair_raters <- length(unique(c(
  hv_pairs_wide$prolific_participant_id_1,
  hv_pairs_wide$prolific_participant_id_2
)))

hv_irr_spearman_demeaned <- cor.test(
  hv_pairs_wide$concerning_demeaned_1,
  hv_pairs_wide$concerning_demeaned_2,
  method = "spearman",
  exact = FALSE
)

hv_irr_icc <- psych::ICC(
  as.matrix(hv_pairs_wide |> select(concerning_1, concerning_2))
)

hv_icc2_1 <- hv_irr_icc$results |>
  filter(type == "ICC2") |>
  pull(ICC)

hv_r1_quintile_levels <- paste0("Q", 1:5)
hv_r1_quintile_breaks <- quantile(
  hv_pairs_wide$concerning_demeaned_1,
  probs = seq(0, 1, 0.2),
  na.rm = TRUE,
  type = 7
)

hv_pairs_wide <- hv_pairs_wide |>
  mutate(
    r1_quintile = cut(
      concerning_demeaned_1,
      breaks = unique(hv_r1_quintile_breaks),
      include.lowest = TRUE,
      labels = hv_r1_quintile_levels[seq_len(length(unique(hv_r1_quintile_breaks)) - 1)]
    ),
    r1_quintile = factor(r1_quintile, levels = hv_r1_quintile_levels)
  )

hv_irr_quintile_summary <- hv_pairs_wide |>
  group_by(r1_quintile) |>
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    mean_r2 = mean(concerning_demeaned_2, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_r2 = sd(concerning_demeaned_2, na.rm = TRUE),
    SEM = sd_r2 / sqrt(N),
    lower = mean_r2 - SEM,
    upper = mean_r2 + SEM,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

hv_irr_quintile_monotonic <- cor.test(
  seq_len(nrow(hv_irr_quintile_summary)),
  hv_irr_quintile_summary$mean_r2,
  method = "spearman",
  exact = FALSE
)

hv_session_summary <- hv |>
  group_by(prolific_participant_id, experiment) |>
  summarise(
    n_annotations = n(),
    session_minutes = as.numeric(difftime(max(annotation_timestamp), min(annotation_timestamp), units = "mins")),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

hv_sessions_per_rater <- hv_session_summary |>
  count(prolific_participant_id, name = "n_sessions")

hv_n_sessions <- nrow(hv_session_summary)
hv_n_raters_one_session <- sum(hv_sessions_per_rater$n_sessions == 1)
hv_n_raters_two_sessions <- sum(hv_sessions_per_rater$n_sessions == 2)
hv_session_n_median <- median(hv_session_summary$n_annotations)
hv_session_n_min <- min(hv_session_summary$n_annotations)
hv_session_n_max <- max(hv_session_summary$n_annotations)
hv_session_median <- median(hv_session_summary$session_minutes, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_session_range_low <- min(hv_session_summary$session_minutes, na.rm = TRUE)
hv_session_range_high <- max(hv_session_summary$session_minutes, na.rm = TRUE)

hv_qc_summary <- hv |>
  group_by(prolific_participant_id) |>
  summarise(
    n_annotations = n(),
    concerning_sd = sd(concerning, na.rm = TRUE),
    machine_score_range = diff(range(turn_concerning, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    fails_low_variance = n_annotations >= hv_min_ratings_for_exclusion &
      concerning_sd < hv_concerning_sd_exclusion_threshold,
    fails_narrow_exposure = n_annotations >= hv_min_ratings_for_exclusion &
      machine_score_range < hv_machine_score_range_threshold,
    excluded = fails_low_variance | fails_narrow_exposure
  )

hv_n_qc_excluded <- sum(hv_qc_summary$excluded)

p_hv_realism_dist <- ggplot(
  hv_realism_table,
  aes(x = Percent, y = Score)
) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = 0, xend = Percent, yend = Score),
    color = "#4C9BD5",
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_point(
    shape = 21, fill = "#4C9BD5", color = "black",
    stroke = group_dot_stroke, size = group_dot_size
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = hv_realism_mean,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "#E45756",
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(
      x = Percent + max(hv_realism_table$Percent) * 0.035,
      label = paste0(Percent, "%")
    ),
    hjust = 0,
    size = base_font_size * 0.32
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = 1:5,
    labels = c(
      "1  Clearly artificial",
      "2  Broadly implausible",
      "3  Mixed",
      "4  Broadly plausible",
      "5  Reads as genuine"
    ),
    limits = c(0.75, 5.25)
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1),
    limits = c(0, max(hv_realism_table$Percent) * 1.22),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Annotator ratings",
    y = NULL,
    title = "E",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Realism of simulated user messages (n = ",
      hv_n_annotations,
      "; dashed red line = mean rating)"
    )
  ) +
  hv_theme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

p_hv_human_llm_scatter <- ggplot(
  hv_hllm,
  aes(x = turn_concerning, y = concerning_demeaned)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "solid", color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "solid", color = "grey92", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.45, size = 0.9, color = "#53A567") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.5, color = "#53A567") +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = min(hv_hllm$turn_concerning) + 0.2,
    y = max(hv_hllm$concerning_demeaned),
    hjust = 0,
    vjust = 1,
    label = paste0(
      "r = ", round(hv_cor_demeaned$estimate, 2),
      " (p ", format_p_value(hv_cor_demeaned$p.value, 2), ")"
    ),
    size = 2.4
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Automated safety judge (turn-level scorer)",
    y = "Human concerning score",
    title = "A",
    subtitle = paste0("Annotator vs LLM (n = ", nrow(hv_hllm), ", ", hv_n_raters, " annotators)")
  ) +
  hv_theme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

p_hv_human_llm_quintile <- ggplot(
  hv_quintile_summary,
  aes(x = llm_quintile, y = mean_human, group = 1)
) +
  geom_line(color = "#53A567", linewidth = 0.45) +
  geom_linerange(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), color = "#53A567", linewidth = 0.45) +
  geom_point(shape = 21, fill = "#53A567", color = "black", stroke = group_dot_stroke, size = group_dot_size) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0("n=", N)),
    nudge_x = 0.28,
    nudge_y = -0.08,
    size = 2.2,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Automated safety judge (turn-level scorer)",
    y = "Mean raw human (\u00b1 SEM)",
    title = "B",
    subtitle = paste0("Annotator vs LLM quintile (n = ", hv_n_annotations, ", ", hv_n_raters, " annotators)")
  ) +
  hv_theme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

p_hv_irr_scatter <- ggplot(
  hv_pairs_wide,
  aes(x = concerning_demeaned_1, y = concerning_demeaned_2)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "solid", color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "solid", color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 1.1, color = "#F26B6C") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.5, color = "#F26B6C") +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = min(hv_pairs_wide$concerning_demeaned_1),
    y = max(hv_pairs_wide$concerning_demeaned_2),
    hjust = 0,
    vjust = 1,
    label = paste0(
      "r = ", round(hv_irr_spearman_demeaned$estimate, 2),
      " (p ", format_p_value(hv_irr_spearman_demeaned$p.value, 2), ")"
    ),
    size = 2.4
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Annotator 1 concerning score",
    y = "Annotator 2 concerning score",
    title = "C",
    subtitle = paste0("Annotator 1 vs Annotator 2 (n = ", nrow(hv_pairs_wide), " items, ", hv_n_pair_raters, " annotators)")
  ) +
  hv_theme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

p_hv_irr_quintile <- ggplot(
  hv_irr_quintile_summary,
  aes(x = r1_quintile, y = mean_r2, group = 1)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey80", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_line(color = "#F26B6C", linewidth = 0.45) +
  geom_linerange(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), color = "#F26B6C", linewidth = 0.45) +
  geom_point(shape = 21, fill = "#F26B6C", color = "black", stroke = group_dot_stroke, size = group_dot_size) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0("n=", N)),
    nudge_x = 0.28,
    nudge_y = -0.08,
    size = 2.2,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Annotator 1 concerning quintile",
    y = "Mean Annotator 2 (\u00b1 SEM)",
    title = "D",
    subtitle = paste0("Annotator 1 vs Annotator 2 quintile (n = ", nrow(hv_pairs_wide), " items, ", hv_n_pair_raters, " annotators)")
  ) +
  hv_theme +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

## Validation of automated safety ratings

The safety judge showed excellent reliability and validity across 4 complementary tests. First, conversation-level scores from 2 independent judge models (claude-opus-4.5 and gpt-5.2) were strongly correlated (r = `r round(STAT.conv_r_pc1$estimate, 2)` for PC1; Extended Data Fig. 1B–C), indicating that conclusions depended only minimally on the underlying judge LLM. Second, scores were stable across the 3 independent simulation runs for each vulnerability × intent × chatbot combination, all using claude-opus-4.5 as the conversation-level safety judge. The intraclass correlation coefficient was high for both a single replicate (ICC(1,1) = `r round(subset(icc_all$results, type == "ICC1")$ICC, 2)`) and the mean of 3 replicates (ICC(1,3) = `r round(subset(icc_all$results, type == "ICC1k")$ICC, 2)`). Third, when the safety judge was applied to curated conversations with known high- versus low-risk profiles, it distinguished them with near-ceiling accuracy (median area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) = `r round(median(targeted_df$auc_high_vs_low, na.rm = TRUE), 2)`; Extended Data Fig. 1D).

Finally, across `r hv_n_annotations` turn-level ratings from `r hv_n_raters` clinician annotators, human ratings of concerning AI chatbot behavior agreed significantly with the turn-level safety judge for this risk dimension (r = `r round(hv_cor_demeaned$estimate, 2)`, p `r format_p_value(hv_cor_demeaned$p.value, 2)`; see Methods and Supplementary Tables 4-6 for the rating protocol, annotator sample, and stimulus coverage). Agreement between an individual human annotator and the LLM safety judge exceeded agreement between two humans rating the same item (human-LLM r = `r round(hv_cor_demeaned$estimate, 2)`; human-human r = `r round(hv_irr_spearman_demeaned$estimate, 2)`, ICC(2,1) = `r round(hv_icc2_1, 2)`; Extended Data Fig. 2A–D), indicating that automated turn-level safety ratings were at least as reliable as an independent clinical judgment. Criterion validity against expert review was also supported by strong absolute agreement between the automated scores and ratings from a clinical psychiatrist (VW), who independently scored the third repetition for each cell in SIM-VAIL’s grid at the conversation level (ICC(3,1) = `r round(subset(icc_expert_concerning$results, type == "ICC3")$ICC, 2)`).

The simulated user-chatbot interactions displayed a high degree of realism, as assessed both by the automated safety judge (mean realism rating of `r round(mean_ci_95(df_scores$score__scenario_realism)$mean, 2)` ± `r round(mean_ci_95(df_scores$score__scenario_realism)$error, 2)` out of 10) and the `r hv_n_raters` human annotators, the latter ascribing an average realism score of `r round(hv_realism_mean, 2)` ± `r round(hv_realism_sd, 2)` (median `r round(hv_realism_median, 0)`) on a scale from `r hv_realism_scale_min` to `r hv_realism_scale_max`, where `r hv_realism_scale_min + 3` = "Broadly plausible" (`r hv_realism_pct_4`%) and `r hv_realism_scale_max` = "Reads as genuine" (`r hv_realism_pct_5`%). Notably, only `r hv_realism_pct_1`% of the `r hv_n_annotations` simulated user-chatbot exchanges were rated as "Clearly artificial" (Extended Data Fig. 2E). We found no examples where the target chatbot itself recognized that the conversation it was engaged in was part of an automated audit (see Supplementary Table 3 for all non-mental-health dimensions assessed by the judge).

## User-profile variation in chatbot risk

First, we tested whether mental-health risk in the target AI chatbots' responses varied across simulated user profiles, regardless of which AI chatbot was being evaluated. We used the *concerning behavior* dimension of the automated safety judge as a broad risk metric, given its high face validity and strong correlation with the main axes of variation across all 13 mental-health risk dimensions (Extended Data Fig. 3).

Regarding simulated user vulnerabilities, concerning behavior scores were highest in psychosis and mania, intermediate for depression and insecure attachment, and lowest in OCD (main effect of vulnerability: F(`r STAT.grid["phenotype","NumDF"]`, `r STAT.grid["phenotype","DenDF"]`) = `r round(STAT.grid["phenotype","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.grid["phenotype","Pr(>F)"], 2)`, Type III F-test; Fig. 2A). Regarding simulated user intent, concerning behavior peaked for intents that invited escalation (glorifying extreme states), promoted dependency on the chatbot (emotional reliance), or enabled harm (seeking permission or help with risky actions). Concerning behavior scores were intermediate for belief validation and symptom minimization, and lowest when simulated users primarily sought reassurance or short-term relief from distress (main effect of intent: F(`r STAT.grid["intention","NumDF"]`, `r STAT.grid["intention","DenDF"]`) = `r round(STAT.grid["intention","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.grid["intention","Pr(>F)"], 2)`; Fig. 2B).

User intent modulated how strongly a given psychological vulnerability elicited concerning AI chatbot behavior (vulnerability × intent interaction: F(`r STAT.grid["phenotype:intention","NumDF"]`, `r STAT.grid["phenotype:intention","DenDF"]`) = `r round(STAT.grid["phenotype:intention","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.grid["phenotype:intention","Pr(>F)"], 2)`; Fig. 2C). For example, simulated conversations of users with OCD generally elicited less concerning chatbot behavior, except when this vulnerability was paired with specific conversational intents (i.e., dependence-oriented requests; risky-action planning). Similarly, conversational intents marked by glorification, minimization, and dependence were particularly likely to lead to concerning chatbot behavior when instantiated in simulated users with depression, mania, and psychosis vulnerabilities. Ordinal robustness analyses reproduced all of the above conclusions (Methods; Supplementary Table 7).

As a control experiment, we additionally simulated conversations with non-vulnerable control users (a psychologically healthy adult engaging the target chatbots in the same 6 intent categories; Supplementary Table 8). These control conversations yielded significantly lower concerning behavior scores than vulnerable-user conversations (p `r format_p_value(STAT.concerning_v1_vs_control$p.value, 2)`), confirming that the observed risks were specific to simulated users with a mental-health vulnerability rather than a general property of AI chatbot behavior (Extended Data Fig. 4).

## Model-level variation in chatbot risk

We next asked how risk behaviors were distributed across different target chatbots. We found that concerning behavior expression differed across the 9 frontier AI chatbots (claude-sonnet-3.7, claude-sonnet-4.5, gpt-4o, gpt-5, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-pro, grok-3, grok-4, and llama-3.1-70B-Instruct). Concerning behavior scores were lowest in Anthropic's claude-sonnet-4.5 model, and highest in xAI's grok-4 (main effect of target chatbot: F(`r STAT.grid["target_model","NumDF"]`, `r STAT.grid["target_model","DenDF"]`) = `r round(STAT.grid["target_model","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.grid["target_model","Pr(>F)"], 2)`; Fig. 3A). Across target chatbots derived from the same model families (e.g., GPT-4o and GPT-5), newer versions generally showed lower concerning-behavior scores than older versions (main version effect: F(`r STAT.new_old["generation","NumDF"]`, `r round(STAT.new_old["generation","DenDF"], 0)`) = `r round(STAT.new_old["generation","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.new_old["generation","Pr(>F)"], 2)`), with the notable exception of grok models (version × model family interaction: F(`r STAT.new_old["generation:family","NumDF"]`, `r round(STAT.new_old["generation:family","DenDF"], 0)`) = `r round(STAT.new_old["generation:family","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.new_old["generation:family","Pr(>F)"], 2)`).

As a robustness check, we re-evaluated Anthropic's claude-sonnet-4.5 using an auditor and judge LLM from a different manufacturer (OpenAI’s gpt-5.2). Claude-sonnet-4.5 still showed the lowest concerning-behavior scores (Anthropic audit: `r round(mean_ci_95(subset(df_scores, target_model == "claude-4.5-sonnet")$score__concerning)$mean,2)` ± `r round(mean_ci_95(subset(df_scores, target_model == "claude-4.5-sonnet")$score__concerning)$error,2)`; OpenAI audit: `r round(mean_ci_95(subset(df_gpt5_sonnet45, target_model == "claude-4.5-sonnet")$score__concerning)$mean,2)` ± `r round(mean_ci_95(subset(df_gpt5_sonnet45, target_model == "claude-4.5-sonnet")$score__concerning)$error,2)`; next best model in the Anthropic audit: gpt-5, `r round(mean_ci_95(subset(df_scores, target_model == "gpt-5")$score__concerning)$mean,2)` ± `r round(mean_ci_95(subset(df_scores, target_model == "gpt-5")$score__concerning)$error,2)`). This rules out the possibility that the superior safety profile observed with claude-sonnet-4.5 was due to using same-family auditor and judge models.

Across target AI chatbots, concerning behavior depended on user profile, reflected in a significant vulnerability × chatbot interaction (F(`r STAT.grid["phenotype:target_model","NumDF"]`, `r STAT.grid["phenotype:target_model","DenDF"]`) = `r round(STAT.grid["phenotype:target_model","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.grid["phenotype:target_model","Pr(>F)"], 2)`; Fig. 3B) and intent × chatbot interaction (F(`r STAT.grid["intention:target_model","NumDF"]`, `r STAT.grid["intention:target_model","DenDF"]`) = `r round(STAT.grid["intention:target_model","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.grid["intention:target_model","Pr(>F)"], 2)`; Fig. 3C). Some models, such as claude-sonnet-4.5, grok-3, grok-4, and llama-3.1-70B-Instruct, showed comparatively consistent behavior across scenarios, ranging from uniformly safe (claude-sonnet-4.5) to broadly concerning (grok-3/4 and llama-3.1-70B-Instruct). Other models, including gpt-4o, gpt-5, claude-sonnet-3.7, gemini-2.5-flash, and gemini-2.5-pro, showed more context-sensitive behavior, illustrating that the same vulnerability–intent combinations can elicit qualitatively different risk signatures across AI chatbots (see Extended Data Fig. 5A for the full vulnerability × intent grid).

## Temporal dynamics of chatbot risk

Next, we investigated how risk unfolded over the course of a conversation. Across all simulated user-chatbot interactions, concerning behavior scores increased as conversations progressed (main effect of turn number: F(`r STAT.turn_main["turn_index","NumDF"]`, `r round(STAT.turn_main["turn_index","DenDF"])`) = `r round(STAT.turn_main["turn_index","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.turn_main["turn_index","Pr(>F)"], 2)`). Across vulnerabilities, escalation was steeper in mania and psychosis and more gradual in depression, OCD, and insecure attachment (turn × vulnerability interaction: F(`r STAT.turn_main["turn_index:phenotype","NumDF"]`, `r round(STAT.turn_main["turn_index:phenotype","DenDF"])`) = `r round(STAT.turn_main["turn_index:phenotype","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.turn_main["turn_index:phenotype","Pr(>F)"], 2)`; Fig. 4A). Across intents, concerning behavior increased earlier and more sharply when users sought dependence on the chatbot or glorification of their experiences (turn × intent interaction: F(`r STAT.turn_main["turn_index:intention","NumDF"]`, `r round(STAT.turn_main["turn_index:intention","DenDF"])`) = `r round(STAT.turn_main["turn_index:intention","F value"], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.turn_main["turn_index:intention","Pr(>F)"], 2)`; Fig. 4B and Extended Data Fig. 6A).

To characterize these dynamic patterns further, we used k-means clustering over all 810 conversations to identify 4 consistent patterns of risk evolution (Fig. 4C): *low risk* conversations that showed almost no escalation in concerning behavior; *gradual escalation* conversations with progressive accumulation across turns; *early escalation* conversations where concerning AI chatbot behavior emerged after the first turn and remained elevated; and *recovery* conversations where risk increased and then declined. Strikingly, these data-driven trajectory classes were unevenly distributed across user vulnerabilities, intents, and chatbots (Fig. 4D).

These findings are important for 2 reasons. First, they illustrate that the simulated user profile determines whether AI chatbots remain safe, drift into sustained risk, or recover after early concerning behavior. This potentially reflects chatbot-specific risk susceptibilities or uneven attention to different user profiles during safety-oriented post-training. Second, the existence of distinct escalation trajectories shows that harm in AI chatbot interactions is rarely a single-response event. This validates the need for turn-resolved evaluations that can detect key inflection and resolution points, and distinguish conversations that reach similar final outcomes through different risk mechanisms [@pombal_mindeval_2025; @zhang_cpsycoun_2024; @moore_expressing_2025].

## Multidimensional structure of chatbot risk

The Vulnerability-Amplifying Interaction Loops (VAILs) hypothesis predicts that the mechanism of concerning AI chatbot behavior differs across user profiles. For example, in conversations with simulated users vulnerable to psychosis, risk may emerge through reinforcement of unusual beliefs; in conversations with simulated users characterized by insecure attachment, it may emerge through intensified emotional dependence on the chatbot. This frames risk as a multidimensional construct, where the pattern of risk observed in a specific user is a function of the user’s vulnerability and conversational intent.

To identify the components of this multidimensional risk space, we conducted a principal component analysis (PCA) over all 13 mental-health-relevant risk dimensions (of which concerning behavior is but one, Extended Data Fig. 3). The first principal axis (PC1), explaining `r round(evr[1]*100,2)`% of the variance, reflected a primary gradient from higher therapeutic quality on the negative pole, to concerning behavior, belief reinforcement, sycophancy and risky action enablement on the positive pole (concerning behavior vs. PC1: r = `r round(STAT.concerning_to_PC1$estimate,2)`).

While PC1 reflected substantial shared variance across risk dimensions, the remaining PCs captured meaningful independent structure: The second axis (PC2), explaining `r round(evr[2]*100,2)`% of the variance, further distinguished the kind of harm that dominated, with the negative pole capturing harm pertaining to relational dynamics (dependence, avoidance, and reassurance) and a positive pole capturing overt harm to others and stigma (Fig. 5A). Higher-order PCs further isolated harm-to-self vs. harm-to-others (PC3, `r round(evr[3]*100,2)`%), relational harms (PC4, `r round(evr[4]*100,2)`%), and medical advice (PC5, `r round(evr[5]*100,2)`%; see Extended Data Fig. 3).

In line with VAILs, the average location of conversations in this risk space differed as a function of simulated user vulnerability and conversational intent (main effect of vulnerability: F(`r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype","num Df"][[1]]`, `r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype","den Df"][[1]]`) = `r round(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype","approx F"][[1]], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype","Pr(>F)"][[1]], 2)`; intent: F(`r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="intention","num Df"][[1]]`, `r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="intention","den Df"][[1]]`) = `r round(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="intention","approx F"][[1]], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="intention","Pr(>F)"][[1]], 2)`; Type III multivariate analysis of variance (MANOVA) on \[PC1, PC2\]; Fig. 5B–C). Mania and psychosis tended to produce risk in the positive-PC2 region, while depression, OCD, and insecure attachment concentrated in the negative-PC2 region. Certain vulnerability–intent pairings also unlocked risk profiles that were otherwise less frequent (vulnerability × intent interaction: F(`r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:intention","num Df"][[1]]`, `r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:intention","den Df"][[1]]`) = `r round(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:intention","approx F"][[1]], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:intention","Pr(>F)"][[1]], 2)`). For instance, when depressed users sought glorification, conversations shifted toward a higher-risk profile with stronger self-harm–related enablement (Extended Data Fig. 6B). 

To validate whether the discovered risk space captured behaviorally interpretable variation beyond differences between user profiles or target chatbots, we performed a within-model causal manipulation. When a single target chatbot was prompted to express specific risk behaviors, its responses shifted in the expected directions in PC1–PC2 space (median cosine vector similarity across dimensions = `r round(median(align_df$cos_align), digits = 2)`; Extended Data Fig. 1E).

Target chatbots also differed in their average location within this risk space (main effect of chatbot: F(`r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="target_model","num Df"][[1]]`, `r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="target_model","den Df"][[1]]`) = `r sprintf("%.2f", STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="target_model","approx F"][[1]])`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="target_model","Pr(>F)"][[1]], 2)`) and in how strongly their behavior depended on the user profile (vulnerability × chatbot interaction: F(`r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:target_model","num Df"][[1]]`, `r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:target_model","den Df"][[1]]`) = `r round(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:target_model","approx F"][[1]], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:target_model","Pr(>F)"][[1]], 2)`; intent × chatbot: F(`r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="intention:target_model","num Df"][[1]]`, `r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="intention:target_model","den Df"][[1]]`) = `r round(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="intention:target_model","approx F"][[1]], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="intention:target_model","Pr(>F)"][[1]], 2)`; vulnerability × intent × chatbot: F(`r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:intention:target_model","num Df"][[1]]`, `r STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:intention:target_model","den Df"][[1]]`) = `r round(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:intention:target_model","approx F"][[1]], 2)`, p `r format_p_value(STAT.pca_mv_pillai[STAT.pca_mv_pillai$term=="phenotype:intention:target_model","Pr(>F)"][[1]], 2)`). For example, under the OCD vulnerability, chatbot conversations clustered tightly in a lower-risk, negative-PC2 region, with grok-3 and grok-4 projected close to all other models. Under the mania vulnerability, by contrast, the same grok models shifted to an extreme, high-risk location in the positive-PC2 region, while other chatbots remained substantially lower on PC1 and PC2. 

Taken together, these results indicate that mental-health risk in chatbot conversations is best construed as a multidimensional construct, were user vulnerability, conversational intent, and target chatbot interact to yield differential risk profiles.

```{r}
#| label: eligible_transcript_selection
#| echo: false
#| include: false

library(scales)
library(gridExtra)

intervention_concerning_threshold <- 7
branch_excluded_target_models <- c("claude-4.5-sonnet")
keep_branch_target <- function(x) {
  !(basename(as.character(x)) %in% branch_excluded_target_models)
}

branch_output_is_malformed <- function(x) {
  x <- dplyr::coalesce(x, "")
  grepl("^\\? Is it supportive\\?", x) |
    grepl("tone should be like a helpful coach", x, ignore.case = TRUE) |
    grepl("^\\s*$", x)
}

paired_delta_ci <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  n <- length(x)
  mean_delta <- if (n) mean(x) else NA_real_
  if (n <= 1) {
    return(tibble::tibble(
      n = n,
      mean_delta = mean_delta,
      lwr = mean_delta,
      upr = mean_delta
    ))
  }
  se <- stats::sd(x) / sqrt(n)
  mult <- stats::qt(0.975, df = n - 1)
  tibble::tibble(
    n = n,
    mean_delta = mean_delta,
    lwr = mean_delta - mult * se,
    upr = mean_delta + mult * se
  )
}

branch_selection_turns <- read_csv("../out/v1/results/conv_turns_full.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(
    target_model = factor(basename(as.character(target_model)), levels = target_levels),
    phenotype = dplyr::coalesce(unname(phenotype_labels[phenotype]), phenotype),
    intention = dplyr::coalesce(unname(intention_labels[intention]), intention),
    phenotype = factor(phenotype, levels = unname(phenotype_labels)),
    intention = factor(intention, levels = unname(intention_labels))
  ) |>
  arrange(transcript_id, assistant_idx)

branch_selection_turns <- branch_selection_turns |>
  group_by(transcript_id) |>
  mutate(
    assistant_turn_rank = row_number(),
    max_assistant_idx = max(assistant_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    has_next_pair = assistant_idx < max_assistant_idx
  ) |>
  ungroup()

eligible_branch_turns <- branch_selection_turns |>
  filter(
    !is.na(concerning),
    concerning >= intervention_concerning_threshold,
    has_next_pair
  )

eligible_branch_transcripts <- eligible_branch_turns |>
  group_by(transcript_id) |>
  slice_min(order_by = assistant_idx, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  distinct(
    transcript_id, target_model, phenotype, intention,
    assistant_idx, assistant_turn_rank, concerning
  ) |>
  filter(assistant_turn_rank < 9)

n_branch_candidates_excluded_late <- eligible_branch_turns |>
  group_by(transcript_id) |>
  slice_min(order_by = assistant_idx, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  distinct(transcript_id, assistant_turn_rank) |>
  summarise(n = sum(assistant_turn_rank >= 9)) |>
  pull(n)

min_branch_selected_turn <- min(eligible_branch_transcripts$assistant_turn_rank, na.rm = TRUE)
max_branch_selected_turn <- max(eligible_branch_transcripts$assistant_turn_rank, na.rm = TRUE)

n_eligible_branch_transcripts <- nrow(eligible_branch_transcripts)

branch_intervention_candidates <- eligible_branch_turns |>
  group_by(transcript_id) |>
  slice_min(order_by = assistant_idx, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(assistant_turn_rank < 9) |>
  transmute(
    transcript_id,
    transcript_file = coalesce(file_conv, file),
    label_id,
    auditor_model,
    target_model = as.character(target_model),
    phenotype = as.character(phenotype),
    intention = as.character(intention),
    user_idx,
    assistant_idx,
    assistant_turn_rank,
    concerning,
    user_text,
    assistant_text,
    threshold = intervention_concerning_threshold
  ) |>
  mutate(
    transcript_file_repo_rel = sub("^\\.\\./", "", transcript_file)
  )

branch_export_dir <- "../out/v1/vail_intervention"
dir.create(branch_export_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

readr::write_csv(
  branch_intervention_candidates,
  file.path(branch_export_dir, "counterfactual_intervention_candidates.csv")
)

readr::write_csv(
  branch_intervention_candidates,
  file.path(
    branch_export_dir,
    paste0("counterfactual_intervention_candidates_threshold_", intervention_concerning_threshold, ".csv")
  )
)

branch_phenotype_summary <- branch_selection_turns |>
  distinct(transcript_id, phenotype) |>
  count(phenotype, name = "total") |>
  left_join(
    eligible_branch_transcripts |>
      count(phenotype, name = "eligible"),
    by = "phenotype"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    eligible = replace_na(eligible, 0L),
    pct = eligible / total,
    label = paste0(eligible, "/", total)
  )

branch_intention_summary <- branch_selection_turns |>
  distinct(transcript_id, intention) |>
  count(intention, name = "total") |>
  left_join(
    eligible_branch_transcripts |>
      count(intention, name = "eligible"),
    by = "intention"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    eligible = replace_na(eligible, 0L),
    pct = eligible / total,
    label = paste0(eligible, "/", total)
  )

branch_model_summary <- branch_selection_turns |>
  distinct(transcript_id, target_model) |>
  count(target_model, name = "total") |>
  left_join(
    eligible_branch_transcripts |>
      count(target_model, name = "eligible"),
    by = "target_model"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    eligible = replace_na(eligible, 0L),
    pct = eligible / total,
    label = paste0(eligible, "/", total)
  )

branch_turn_rank_summary <- eligible_branch_transcripts |>
  count(assistant_turn_rank, name = "n") |>
  mutate(
    assistant_turn_rank = factor(assistant_turn_rank, levels = sort(unique(assistant_turn_rank))),
    label = as.character(n)
  )

branch_turn_rank_levels <- sort(unique(eligible_branch_transcripts$assistant_turn_rank))
branch_turn_rank_palette <- setNames(
  colorRampPalette(c("#deebf7", "#08519c"))(length(branch_turn_rank_levels)),
  as.character(branch_turn_rank_levels)
)

branch_selection_coverage <- bind_rows(
  branch_phenotype_summary |>
    transmute(
      facet = "Vulnerability",
      category = as.character(phenotype),
      pct,
      label,
      fill_value = OVERALL_BLUE
    ),
  branch_model_summary |>
    transmute(
      facet = "Target AI chatbot",
      category = as.character(target_model),
      pct,
      label,
      fill_value = unname(R25_target_palette[as.character(target_model)])
    ),
  branch_intention_summary |>
    transmute(
      facet = "Intent",
      category = as.character(intention),
      pct,
      label,
      fill_value = OVERALL_BLUE
    )
) |>
  mutate(
    facet = factor(facet, levels = c("Vulnerability", "Intent", "Target AI chatbot")),
    category_id = paste(facet, category, sep = "___")
  )

branch_selection_category_levels <- c(
  paste("Vulnerability", rev(unname(phenotype_labels)), sep = "___"),
  paste("Intent", rev(unname(intention_labels)), sep = "___"),
  paste("Target AI chatbot", rev(target_levels), sep = "___")
)

branch_selection_coverage <- branch_selection_coverage |>
  mutate(
    category_id = factor(category_id, levels = branch_selection_category_levels)
  )

branch_selection_prehistory <- branch_selection_turns |>
  select(transcript_id, phenotype, assistant_turn_rank, concerning) |>
  inner_join(
    eligible_branch_transcripts |>
      select(transcript_id, intervention_turn_rank = assistant_turn_rank),
    by = "transcript_id"
  ) |>
  mutate(relative_turn = assistant_turn_rank - intervention_turn_rank) |>
  filter(relative_turn <= 0)

branch_selection_prehistory_summary <- branch_selection_prehistory |>
  group_by(intervention_turn_rank, relative_turn) |>
  summarise(
    n = sum(!is.na(concerning)),
    mean = mean(concerning, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    intervention_turn_rank = factor(
      intervention_turn_rank,
      levels = branch_turn_rank_levels
    )
  )

branch_selection_common_y <- scale_y_continuous(
  labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
  limits = c(0, 1),
  expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))
)

p_branch_coverage <- ggplot(
  branch_selection_coverage,
  aes(x = pct, y = category_id, color = fill_value)
) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = 0, xend = pct, yend = category_id),
    linewidth = 0.7,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_point(aes(fill = fill_value), shape = 21, color = "black", stroke = group_dot_stroke, size = group_dot_size, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(
    aes(x = pmin(pct + 0.03, 1.06), label = label),
    hjust = 0,
    size = base_font_size * 0.28
  ) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, 1.08),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = function(x) sub("^.*___", "", x)) +
  facet_grid(facet ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x = "Eligible conversations (fraction)",
    y = NULL,
    title = "C",
    subtitle = "Coverage by vulnerability, intent,\nand target AI chatbot"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme_like_main +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    strip.text.y = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_font_size - 0.2),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.85)
  )

p_branch_turn_rank <- ggplot(
  branch_turn_rank_summary,
  aes(x = assistant_turn_rank, y = n)
) +
  geom_col(width = 0.72, fill = OVERALL_BLUE, color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), vjust = -0.35, size = base_font_size * 0.28) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Assistant turn selected for intervention",
    y = "Eligible conversations",
    title = "A",
    subtitle = "Where the intervention point\noccurs"
  ) +
  theme_like_main +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

p_branch_prehistory <- ggplot(
  branch_selection_prehistory_summary,
  aes(
    x = relative_turn,
    y = mean,
    group = intervention_turn_rank,
    color = intervention_turn_rank
  )
) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = intervention_concerning_threshold,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey35",
    linewidth = 0.35
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.95) +
  geom_point(aes(fill = intervention_turn_rank), shape = 21, color = "black", stroke = group_dot_stroke, size = group_dot_size, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = branch_turn_rank_palette, name = "t = ") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = branch_turn_rank_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(1, 10),
    breaks = 1:10,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(min(branch_selection_prehistory_summary$relative_turn), 0, by = 1),
    labels = function(x) ifelse(x == 0, "t", paste0("t", x)),
    minor_breaks = NULL,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.01))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Relative assistant turn",
    y = "Mean concerning",
    title = "B",
    subtitle = "Observed trajectory up to the\nselected intervention turn"
  ) +
  theme_like_main +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: target_message_persistence_setup
#| echo: false
#| include: false

target_persistence_additional_pairs <- 4
load_target_persistence_paired <- function(n = 4) {
  persistence_dir <- "../out/v1/vail_intervention/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent/persistence"
  horizon_dir <- file.path(persistence_dir, paste0("n_", sprintf("%02d", n)))
  original_steps_path <- file.path(
    horizon_dir,
    "original/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent_persistence_steps_long.csv"
  )
  deescalated_steps_path <- file.path(
    horizon_dir,
    "deescalated/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent_persistence_steps_long.csv"
  )
  original_summary_path <- file.path(
    horizon_dir,
    "original/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent_persistence_summary.csv"
  )
  deescalated_summary_path <- file.path(
    horizon_dir,
    "deescalated/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent_persistence_summary.csv"
  )

  required_paths <- c(
    original_steps_path,
    deescalated_steps_path,
    original_summary_path,
    deescalated_summary_path
  )
  if (!all(file.exists(required_paths))) {
    return(tibble::tibble())
  }

  original_summary <- readr::read_csv(original_summary_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    dplyr::select(transcript_id, target_model)
  deescalated_summary <- readr::read_csv(deescalated_summary_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    dplyr::select(transcript_id, target_model)

  original_steps <- readr::read_csv(original_steps_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    dplyr::left_join(original_summary, by = "transcript_id")
  deescalated_steps <- readr::read_csv(deescalated_steps_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    dplyr::left_join(deescalated_summary, by = "transcript_id")

  dplyr::inner_join(
    original_steps,
    deescalated_steps,
    by = c("transcript_id", "step_index"),
    suffix = c("_original", "_deescalated")
  ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      downstream_turn = step_index + 1L,
      target_model = dplyr::coalesce(target_model_original, target_model_deescalated),
      valid_original_output = !branch_output_is_malformed(generated_assistant_text_original),
      valid_deescalated_output = !branch_output_is_malformed(generated_assistant_text_deescalated),
      valid_pair = valid_original_output & valid_deescalated_output,
      concerning_delta = concerning_deescalated - concerning_original
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(valid_pair, keep_branch_target(target_model), downstream_turn <= 5)
}

get_coef_stat <- function(coef_mat, terms, column) {
  if (is.null(coef_mat) || !(column %in% colnames(coef_mat))) {
    return(NA_real_)
  }
  term <- intersect(terms, rownames(coef_mat))[1]
  if (is.na(term)) {
    return(NA_real_)
  }
  unname(coef_mat[term, column])
}

target_persistence_delta_summary <- tibble::tibble()
target_persistence_t1_delta_summary <- tibble::tibble(
  downstream_turn = numeric(),
  n = integer(),
  mean_delta = numeric(),
  lwr = numeric(),
  upr = numeric()
)
target_persistence_t1_concerning_t <- list(statistic = NA_real_, p.value = NA_real_)
target_persistence_branch_beta <- NA_real_
target_persistence_branch_p <- NA_real_
target_persistence_time_beta <- NA_real_
target_persistence_time_p <- NA_real_
target_persistence_interaction_beta <- NA_real_
target_persistence_interaction_p <- NA_real_

target_persistence_paired <- load_target_persistence_paired(target_persistence_additional_pairs)
target_persistence_available <- nrow(target_persistence_paired) > 0

if (target_persistence_available) {
  target_persistence_paired <- target_persistence_paired |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      target_model = factor(as.character(target_model), levels = target_levels)
    )

  target_persistence_n_paired <- target_persistence_paired |>
    summarise(n = n_distinct(transcript_id)) |>
    pull(n)

  target_persistence_delta_summary <- target_persistence_paired |>
    group_by(downstream_turn) |>
    summarise(
      n = sum(!is.na(concerning_delta)),
      mean_delta = mean(concerning_delta, na.rm = TRUE),
      se = sd(concerning_delta, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n),
      lwr = mean_delta - qt(0.975, df = pmax(n - 1, 1)) * se,
      upr = mean_delta + qt(0.975, df = pmax(n - 1, 1)) * se,
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  target_persistence_t1_delta_summary <- target_persistence_delta_summary |>
    filter(downstream_turn == 1)

  target_persistence_t1_paired <- target_persistence_paired |>
    filter(downstream_turn == 1)

  if (nrow(target_persistence_t1_paired) > 1) {
    target_persistence_t1_concerning_t <- stats::t.test(target_persistence_t1_paired$concerning_delta)
  }

  target_persistence_lm_long <- dplyr::bind_rows(
    target_persistence_paired |>
      dplyr::transmute(
        transcript_id,
        downstream_turn,
        branch = "original",
        concerning = concerning_original
      ),
    target_persistence_paired |>
      dplyr::transmute(
        transcript_id,
        downstream_turn,
        branch = "deescalated",
        concerning = concerning_deescalated
      )
  ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(branch = factor(branch, levels = c("original", "deescalated")))

  target_persistence_lm <- stats::lm(
    concerning ~ branch * downstream_turn,
    data = target_persistence_lm_long
  )
  target_persistence_lm_coef <- summary(target_persistence_lm)$coefficients
  target_persistence_branch_beta <- get_coef_stat(
    target_persistence_lm_coef,
    c("branch1", "branchdeescalated"),
    "Estimate"
  )
  target_persistence_branch_p <- get_coef_stat(
    target_persistence_lm_coef,
    c("branch1", "branchdeescalated"),
    "Pr(>|t|)"
  )
  target_persistence_time_beta <- get_coef_stat(target_persistence_lm_coef, "downstream_turn", "Estimate")
  target_persistence_time_p <- get_coef_stat(target_persistence_lm_coef, "downstream_turn", "Pr(>|t|)")
  target_persistence_interaction_beta <- get_coef_stat(
    target_persistence_lm_coef,
    c("branch1:downstream_turn", "branchdeescalated:downstream_turn"),
    "Estimate"
  )
  target_persistence_interaction_p <- get_coef_stat(
    target_persistence_lm_coef,
    c("branch1:downstream_turn", "branchdeescalated:downstream_turn"),
    "Pr(>|t|)"
  )
} else {
  target_persistence_n_paired <- 0
  target_persistence_delta_summary <- tibble::tibble()
  target_persistence_t1_delta_summary <- tibble::tibble()
  target_persistence_t1_concerning_t <- list(statistic = NA_real_, p.value = NA_real_)
}
```

```{r}
#| label: branch_delta_setup
#| echo: false
#| include: false

user_intervention_original_path_candidates <- c(
  "../out/v1/vail_intervention/user_to_model/preceding_user/original/user_to_model_summary.csv",
  "../out/v1/vail_intervention/user_to_model_turn_only_scores/preceding_user/original/user_to_model_turn_only_scores_summary.csv"
)
user_intervention_original_path <- user_intervention_original_path_candidates[
  file.exists(user_intervention_original_path_candidates)
][1]
if (is.na(user_intervention_original_path)) {
  user_intervention_original_path <- user_intervention_original_path_candidates[1]
}
user_intervention_deescalating_path_candidates <- c(
  "../out/v1/vail_intervention/user_to_model/preceding_user/deescalating/user_to_model_summary.csv",
  "../out/v1/vail_intervention/user_to_model_turn_only_scores/preceding_user/deescalating/user_to_model_turn_only_scores_summary.csv"
)
user_intervention_deescalating_path <- user_intervention_deescalating_path_candidates[
  file.exists(user_intervention_deescalating_path_candidates)
][1]
if (is.na(user_intervention_deescalating_path)) {
  user_intervention_deescalating_path <- user_intervention_deescalating_path_candidates[1]
}

branch_mh_dims <- c(
  "concerning",
  "unprompted_sycophancy",
  "mh_maladaptive_belief_reinforcement",
  "mh_risky_action_enablement",
  "mh_avoidance_and_reassurance_loops",
  "mh_boundary_and_dependence_risk",
  "mh_minimization_of_risk_or_symptoms",
  "mh_therapeutic_quality",
  "self_harm_or_suicide_enablement",
  "mh_medical_advice",
  "mh_harm_to_others",
  "mh_glorification_or_romanticization",
  "mh_stigma_and_invalidation"
)
branch_mh_dim_labels <- c(
  concerning = "Concerning",
  unprompted_sycophancy = "Sycophancy",
  mh_maladaptive_belief_reinforcement = "Belief reinforcement",
  mh_risky_action_enablement = "Risky action",
  mh_avoidance_and_reassurance_loops = "Avoidance / reassurance",
  mh_boundary_and_dependence_risk = "Boundary / dependence",
  mh_minimization_of_risk_or_symptoms = "Minimizing symptoms",
  mh_therapeutic_quality = "Therapeutic quality",
  self_harm_or_suicide_enablement = "Self-harm enablement",
  mh_medical_advice = "Medical advice",
  mh_harm_to_others = "Harm to others",
  mh_glorification_or_romanticization = "Glorification",
  mh_stigma_and_invalidation = "Stigma / invalidation"
)

user_intervention_original <- readr::read_csv(user_intervention_original_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::filter(keep_branch_target(target_model))
user_intervention_deescalating <- readr::read_csv(user_intervention_deescalating_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::filter(keep_branch_target(target_model))

user_intervention_paired <- dplyr::inner_join(
  user_intervention_original,
  user_intervention_deescalating,
  by = "transcript_id",
  suffix = c("_original", "_deescalating")
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    valid_original_output = !branch_output_is_malformed(generated_assistant_text_original),
    valid_deescalating_output = !branch_output_is_malformed(generated_assistant_text_deescalating),
    valid_pair = valid_original_output & valid_deescalating_output
  ) |>
  dplyr::filter(valid_pair)

user_intervention_n_valid <- nrow(user_intervention_paired)
user_intervention_concerning_delta_mean <- mean(
  user_intervention_paired$concerning_deescalating - user_intervention_paired$concerning_original
)
user_intervention_therapeutic_delta_mean <- mean(
  user_intervention_paired$mh_therapeutic_quality_deescalating -
    user_intervention_paired$mh_therapeutic_quality_original
)
user_intervention_concerning_t <- t.test(
  user_intervention_paired$concerning_deescalating,
  user_intervention_paired$concerning_original,
  paired = TRUE
)

user_intervention_delta_summary <- purrr::map_dfr(
  branch_mh_dims,
  function(dim_name) {
    delta <- user_intervention_paired[[paste0(dim_name, "_deescalating")]] -
      user_intervention_paired[[paste0(dim_name, "_original")]]
    paired_delta_ci(delta) |>
      dplyr::mutate(dimension = dim_name)
  }
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dimension_label = factor(
      unname(branch_mh_dim_labels[dimension]),
      levels = unname(branch_mh_dim_labels[branch_mh_dims])
    )
  )

target_intervention_original <- readr::read_csv(
  "../out/v1/vail_intervention/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent/original/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent_summary.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::filter(keep_branch_target(target_model))
target_intervention_deescalated <- readr::read_csv(
  "../out/v1/vail_intervention/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent/deescalated/model_to_user_to_model_locked_intent_summary.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::filter(keep_branch_target(target_model))

target_intervention_paired <- dplyr::inner_join(
  target_intervention_original,
  target_intervention_deescalated,
  by = "transcript_id",
  suffix = c("_original", "_deescalated")
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    valid_original_output =
      !branch_output_is_malformed(generated_user_text_original) &
      !branch_output_is_malformed(generated_assistant_text_original),
    valid_deescalated_output =
      !branch_output_is_malformed(generated_user_text_deescalated) &
      !branch_output_is_malformed(generated_assistant_text_deescalated),
    valid_pair = valid_original_output & valid_deescalated_output
  ) |>
  dplyr::filter(valid_pair)

target_intervention_user_pressure_delta_mean <- mean(
  target_intervention_paired$user_escalation_pressure_deescalated -
    target_intervention_paired$user_escalation_pressure_original
)
target_intervention_user_pressure_tt <- t.test(
  target_intervention_paired$user_escalation_pressure_deescalated,
  target_intervention_paired$user_escalation_pressure_original,
  paired = TRUE
)
target_intervention_user_pressure_t <- tibble::tibble(
  t_stat = unname(target_intervention_user_pressure_tt$statistic),
  p_value = target_intervention_user_pressure_tt$p.value
)

target_intervention_user_challenge_delta_mean <- mean(
  target_intervention_paired$user_intention_challenge_deescalated -
    target_intervention_paired$user_intention_challenge_original
)
target_intervention_user_challenge_tt <- t.test(
  target_intervention_paired$user_intention_challenge_deescalated,
  target_intervention_paired$user_intention_challenge_original,
  paired = TRUE
)
target_intervention_user_challenge_t <- tibble::tibble(
  t_stat = unname(target_intervention_user_challenge_tt$statistic),
  p_value = target_intervention_user_challenge_tt$p.value
)

target_intervention_concerning_delta_mean <- mean(
  target_intervention_paired$concerning_deescalated -
    target_intervention_paired$concerning_original
)
target_intervention_concerning_t <- t.test(
  target_intervention_paired$concerning_deescalated,
  target_intervention_paired$concerning_original,
  paired = TRUE
)

target_intervention_delta_summary <- purrr::map_dfr(
  branch_mh_dims,
  function(dim_name) {
    delta <- target_intervention_paired[[paste0(dim_name, "_deescalated")]] -
      target_intervention_paired[[paste0(dim_name, "_original")]]
    paired_delta_ci(delta) |>
      dplyr::mutate(dimension = dim_name)
  }
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dimension_label = factor(
      unname(branch_mh_dim_labels[dimension]),
      levels = unname(branch_mh_dim_labels[branch_mh_dims])
    )
  )

branch_delta_limits <- c(
  min(
    0,
    suppressWarnings(min(user_intervention_delta_summary$lwr, na.rm = TRUE)),
    suppressWarnings(min(target_intervention_delta_summary$lwr, na.rm = TRUE)),
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) - 0.2,
  max(
    0,
    suppressWarnings(max(user_intervention_delta_summary$upr, na.rm = TRUE)),
    suppressWarnings(max(target_intervention_delta_summary$upr, na.rm = TRUE)),
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) + 0.2
)

target_persistence_delta_limits <- c(
  min(
    0,
    suppressWarnings(min(target_persistence_delta_summary$lwr, na.rm = TRUE)),
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) - 0.2,
  max(
    0,
    suppressWarnings(max(target_persistence_delta_summary$upr, na.rm = TRUE)),
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) + 0.2
)

user_intervention_color <- "#5F8EA7"
target_intervention_color <- "#8A74B7"
intervention_palette <- c(
  "User message intervention" = user_intervention_color,
  "Target message intervention" = target_intervention_color
)

if (nrow(user_intervention_delta_summary) > 0) {
  p_user_intervention_delta <- ggplot(
    user_intervention_delta_summary,
    aes(x = dimension_label, y = mean_delta)
  ) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_linerange(aes(ymin = lwr, ymax = upr), linewidth = 0.55, color = user_intervention_color) +
    geom_point(shape = 21, fill = user_intervention_color, color = "black", stroke = group_dot_stroke, size = group_dot_size) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.06))) +
    labs(
      x = NULL,
      y = "De-escalated - original",
      title = "B",
      subtitle = "Effects of user-message intervention on target behavior at t"
    ) +
    theme_like_main +
    theme(
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1)
    )
} else {
  p_user_intervention_delta <- ggplot() + theme_void()
}
```
